Welcome, Jubili

Jubili, before we touch a single line of code, I want to say this clearly: your research instinct was strong from the beginning.

You were already thinking like a political economist: start from a real policy shock, map it into local economies, and connect it to political outcomes.

That is exactly the right blueprint.

What we are doing in this workbook is not replacing your project. We are upgrading its measurement precision and interpretation discipline so your results can stand up in serious academic conversation.

How to use this notebook in a live session

For each section: read the framing note, run the code chunk, and answer the checkpoint question before moving on.

Learning goals

By the end, you should be able to explain the exposure formula, diagnose customs-exit geography issues, separate level from per-worker intensity, and interpret a regression ladder with proper caution.

1. Environment check

Framing

This first block is boring on purpose. Professional work starts with reproducibility.

If this block fails, we stop and fix setup before we interpret anything.

if (file.exists("retaliatory-tariff.Rproj")) {
  project_root <- "."
} else if (file.exists("../retaliatory-tariff.Rproj")) {
  project_root <- ".."
} else {
  stop("Could not find retaliatory-tariff.Rproj. Open this workbook from inside the project folder.")
}

setwd(project_root)
knitr::opts_knit$set(root.dir = normalizePath(getwd()))
cat("Working directory:", normalizePath(getwd()), "\n")
## Working directory: /Users/ian/gemini_playground/tutoring/Jubili_RCoding/retaliatory-tariff
packages <- c(
  "readr", "dplyr", "tidyr", "stringr", "ggplot2", "scales",
  "knitr", "fixest", "modelsummary", "sf", "tigris"
)

missing_pkgs <- packages[!vapply(packages, requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE, FUN.VALUE = logical(1))]
if (length(missing_pkgs) > 0) {
  stop("Install missing packages first:\n", paste0("install.packages('", missing_pkgs, "')", collapse = "\n"))
}

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(readr)
  library(dplyr)
  library(tidyr)
  library(stringr)
  library(ggplot2)
  library(scales)
  library(knitr)
})

Checkpoint

What does it mean for a project to be reproducible?

2. Core question and formula

Research question

Did retaliatory tariffs land more heavily in politically salient places?

Framing

This question is clean and worth keeping exactly as-is.

Working formula

\[ \text{Exposure}_{ct} = \sum_i \left(\frac{L_{cit}}{L_{ct}}\right) \times \left(X^{US}_{it} \times \tau_{it}\right) \]

Where:

  • \(L_{cit}/L_{ct}\): county employment share in sector \(i\)
  • \(X^{US}_{it} \times \tau_{it}\): national tariff-exposed export shock in sector \(i\)
  • Summing across sectors gives county-year exposure

Teaching takeaway

“If you can explain each symbol in one sentence without looking at notes, you own the model.”

Checkpoint

What is the shift term? What is the share term?

3. Respectful diagnosis of the original approach

Framing

We are going to critique the old pipeline without disrespecting it.

Key strengths:

  • Strong instinct for combining tariffs, trade, and local structure
  • Real data engineering effort
  • Willingness to map and test

Key corrections:

  • Export geography was tied to customs-exit location
  • Local production exposure can be misassigned when exit geography is used as production geography

3.1 Legacy coverage check

legacy <- read_csv("data/tools/export_with_fips.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

legacy_fips <- legacy %>%
  mutate(fips_code = str_replace_all(fips_code, ";", ",")) %>%
  separate_rows(fips_code, sep = ",") %>%
  mutate(
    fips_code = str_trim(fips_code),
    county_fips = str_extract(fips_code, "[0-9]{5}")
  )

legacy_summary <- legacy_fips %>%
  summarise(
    rows = n(),
    unique_districts = n_distinct(District),
    unique_counties = n_distinct(county_fips, na.rm = TRUE),
    missing_fips_share = mean(is.na(county_fips))
  )

kable(legacy_summary, digits = 3)
rows unique_districts unique_counties missing_fips_share
100259 46 53 0.035

Teaching takeaway

“This isn’t about blaming old code. This is about making sure the geography in our data matches the geography in our claim.”

Checkpoint

If a car is manufactured in Michigan and shipped out through Texas, where is the production-side labor shock?

4. Load the upgraded county panel

Framing

Now we move to the corrected structure that separates national shocks from local workforce composition.

panel <- read_csv(
  "data/public/derived/county_tariff_exposure_panel_with_votes_2017_2023.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
) %>%
  mutate(
    county_fips = str_pad(as.character(county_fips), 5, pad = "0"),
    year = as.integer(year)
  )

panel %>%
  summarise(
    rows = n(),
    years = n_distinct(year),
    counties = n_distinct(county_fips),
    min_year = min(year, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_year = max(year, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  kable()
rows years counties min_year max_year
22659 7 3253 2017 2023 
panel %>%
  group_by(year) %>%
  summarise(counties = n_distinct(county_fips), .groups = "drop") %>%
  kable()
year counties
2017 3235
2018 3235
2019 3237
2020 3238
2021 3238
2022 3239
2023 3237

Checkpoint

Why is broad county coverage a design-quality issue, not just a data-quality issue?

5. Reconstruct exposure from components (trust-building step)

Framing

If we cannot rebuild the final exposure from its components, we should not trust the pipeline.

This section is one of the most important in the whole workbook.

components <- read_csv(
  "data/public/derived/county_industry_tariff_panel_2017_2023.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
) %>%
  mutate(
    county_fips = str_pad(as.character(county_fips), 5, pad = "0"),
    year = as.integer(year)
  )

5.1 Example county: Hoonah-Angoon Census Area

focus <- components %>%
  filter(str_detect(county_name, "Hoonah-Angoon")) %>%
  distinct(county_fips, county_name) %>%
  slice(1)

focus_fips <- focus$county_fips[[1]]
focus_name <- focus$county_name[[1]]
focus_year <- max(panel$year, na.rm = TRUE)

cat("County:", focus_name, "| FIPS:", focus_fips, "| Year:", focus_year, "\n")
## County: Hoonah-Angoon Census Area, Alaska | FIPS: 02105 | Year: 2023
focus_tbl <- components %>%
  filter(county_fips == focus_fips, year == focus_year) %>%
  mutate(rebuilt_component = emp_share * export_value_usd * tariff_rate_mean) %>%
  select(naics2c, emp_share, export_value_usd, tariff_rate_mean,
         county_tariff_component_mean, rebuilt_component) %>%
  arrange(desc(rebuilt_component))

kable(slice_head(focus_tbl, n = 12), digits = 5)
naics2c emp_share export_value_usd tariff_rate_mean county_tariff_component_mean rebuilt_component
22 0.03361 0 0 0 0
23 0.05602 0 0 0 0
44-45 0.22409 0 0 0 0
48-49 0.15966 0 0 0 0
53 0.00840 0 0 0 0
62 0.15126 0 0 0 0
71 0.15966 0 0 0 0
72 0.08123 0 0 0 0 
rebuilt <- components %>%
  group_by(county_fips, year) %>%
  summarise(rebuilt_exposure = sum(county_tariff_component_mean, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

consistency <- panel %>%
  select(county_fips, year, county_tariff_exposure_mean) %>%
  inner_join(rebuilt, by = c("county_fips", "year")) %>%
  mutate(abs_diff = abs(county_tariff_exposure_mean - rebuilt_exposure))

consistency %>%
  summarise(
    median_abs_diff = median(abs_diff, na.rm = TRUE),
    max_abs_diff = max(abs_diff, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  kable(digits = 6)
median_abs_diff max_abs_diff
0 1e-06

Teaching takeaway

“This is the moment where we stop hoping and start verifying.”

Checkpoint

Why do we trust a measure more after reconstruction checks?

6. NAICS audit for problematic entries

Framing

A lot of project pain comes from quiet coding mismatches, not dramatic coding errors.

This scanner helps catch quiet NAICS issues before they pollute regressions.

emp_panel <- read_csv(
  "data/public/derived/county_industry_emp_share_2017_2023.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
) %>%
  mutate(
    county_fips = str_pad(as.character(county_fips), 5, pad = "0"),
    naics2c = as.character(naics2c),
    valid_naics = str_detect(naics2c, "^(00|[0-9]{2}|31-33|44-45|48-49|99)$"),
    emp = as.numeric(emp),
    emp_share = as.numeric(emp_share)
  )

naics_issues <- emp_panel %>%
  mutate(
    issue = case_when(
      !valid_naics ~ "bad_naics_format",
      is.na(emp) ~ "missing_emp",
      is.na(emp_share) ~ "missing_emp_share",
      !is.finite(emp_share) ~ "nonfinite_emp_share",
      TRUE ~ "ok"
    )
  ) %>%
  filter(issue != "ok") %>%
  count(year, issue, naics2c, sort = TRUE, name = "rows")

kable(slice_head(naics_issues, n = 25))
year issue naics2c rows
2017 missing_emp_share 21 1
2017 missing_emp_share 44-45 1
2017 missing_emp_share 48-49 1
2017 missing_emp_share 56 1
2017 missing_emp_share 62 1
2017 missing_emp_share 72 1 
dir.create("output/analysis/tables", recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
write_csv(naics_issues, "output/analysis/tables/naics_problem_entries_client.csv")
cat("Wrote output/analysis/tables/naics_problem_entries_client.csv\n")
## Wrote output/analysis/tables/naics_problem_entries_client.csv

Checkpoint

What is one NAICS cleaning issue that could silently break your model?

7. Level vs per-worker intensity

Framing

This is where we solve the scale problem directly.

A big county can have high total exposure but low intensity. A smaller county can have lower total exposure but much higher per-worker burden.

Both views matter.

latest_year <- max(panel$year, na.rm = TRUE)
latest <- panel %>% filter(year == latest_year)

top_level <- latest %>%
  arrange(desc(county_tariff_exposure_mean)) %>%
  select(county_name, county_fips, county_tariff_exposure_mean,
         county_tariff_exposure_per_worker, county_emp_total) %>%
  slice_head(n = 12)

kable(top_level, digits = 3, caption = "Top counties by exposure level")
Top counties by exposure level
county_name county_fips county_tariff_exposure_mean county_tariff_exposure_per_worker county_emp_total
Los Angeles County, California 06037 9015205583 2260.733 3987736
Harris County, Texas 48201 7324700221 3356.622 2182164
Cook County, Illinois 17031 5270854463 2114.832 2492328
Orange County, California 06059 4322978386 2734.716 1580778
Maricopa County, Arizona 04013 3992995255 1979.684 2016986
Dallas County, Texas 48113 3630425950 2201.990 1648702

Tarrant County, Texas 48439 2970926156 3367.294 882289

Alameda County, California 06001 2742939892 3661.530 749124
Wayne County, Michigan 26163 2707111837 4067.432 665558
King County, Washington 53033 2600370709 1991.176 1305947 
top_intensity <- latest %>%
  arrange(desc(county_tariff_exposure_per_worker)) %>%
  select(county_name, county_fips, county_tariff_exposure_mean,
         county_tariff_exposure_per_worker, county_emp_total) %>%
  slice_head(n = 12)

kable(top_intensity, digits = 3, caption = "Top counties by exposure per worker")
Top counties by exposure per worker

Checkpoint

Which counties move the most when you switch from level to per-worker ranking, and what does that teach you?

8. Mapping in R (static diagnostic view)

Framing

Good maps are not decoration. They are diagnostics.

If your map is unclear, your interpretation usually is too.

options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)

counties_sf <- tigris::counties(cb = TRUE, year = 2023, class = "sf") %>%
  sf::st_as_sf() %>%
  select(GEOID, geometry)

map_df <- counties_sf %>%
  left_join(
    latest %>% transmute(GEOID = county_fips, exposure_per_worker = county_tariff_exposure_per_worker),
    by = "GEOID"
  ) %>%
  filter(!str_sub(GEOID, 1, 2) %in% c("02", "15", "60", "66", "69", "72", "78"))

ggplot(map_df) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = exposure_per_worker), color = NA) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "C", trans = "log1p", na.value = "grey92") +
  labs(
    title = paste0("County tariff exposure per worker (", latest_year, ")"),
    subtitle = "Log-scaled fill to reduce outlier compression",
    fill = "Exposure / worker"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(panel.grid = element_blank())

Checkpoint

Why do we log-scale this map instead of plotting raw values directly?

9. Regression ladder (HC1, FE, clustered)

Framing

We are not chasing significance. We are checking whether relationships hold under stronger assumptions and more realistic uncertainty.

suppressPackageStartupMessages({
  library(fixest)
  library(modelsummary)
})

reg_df <- panel %>%
  mutate(y = log1p(county_tariff_exposure_mean)) %>%
  filter(is.finite(y), !is.na(gop_share_20), !is.na(swingness_20), !is.na(tariffed_emp_share))

m1 <- feols(y ~ gop_share_20 + swingness_20 + tariffed_emp_share,
            data = reg_df,
            vcov = "hetero")

m2 <- feols(y ~ gop_share_20 + swingness_20 + tariffed_emp_share | state_name,
            data = reg_df %>% filter(!is.na(state_name), state_name != ""),
            vcov = "hetero")

m3 <- feols(y ~ gop_share_20 + swingness_20 + tariffed_emp_share | state_name,
            data = reg_df %>% filter(!is.na(state_name), state_name != ""),
            vcov = ~state_name)

modelsummary(
  list(
    "M1 HC1" = m1,
    "M2 + State FE (HC1)" = m2,
    "M3 + State FE (Clustered)" = m3
  ),
  stars = FALSE,
  fmt = 3,
  output = "markdown"
)
M1 HC1 M2 + State FE (HC1) M3 + State FE (Clustered)
(Intercept) 15.528
(0.253)
gop_share_20 -5.655 -5.152 -5.152
(0.255) (0.267) (1.171)
swingness_20 3.312 3.152 3.152
(0.177) (0.178) (0.711)
tariffed_emp_share 14.952 14.190 14.190
(0.277) (0.274) (1.737)
Num.Obs. 21717 21677 21677
R2 0.292 0.365 0.365
R2 Adj. 0.292 0.363 0.363
R2 Within 0.236 0.236
R2 Within Adj. 0.236 0.236
AIC 115733.4 113281.4 113281.4
BIC 115765.3 113696.6 113696.6
RMSE 3.47 3.29 3.29
Std.Errors Heteroskedasticity-robust Heteroskedasticity-robust by: state_name
FE: state_name X X

Teaching takeaway

“When clustered uncertainty widens intervals, that is not failure. That is honesty.”

Checkpoint

What changed the most between M1 and M3: coefficient signs, magnitudes, or confidence intervals?

10. Interpretation discipline

When you write results, keep the sequence consistent: what changed in the model, what stayed stable, where uncertainty widened, what can be claimed as association, and what cannot be claimed causally.

Writing template

“Counties with higher tariff exposure show [direction] association with [outcome] in baseline models. This pattern [persists/attenuates] under state fixed effects and clustered inference. We interpret the evidence as county-level association in predicted vulnerability, not direct proof of realized income loss or causal effect.”

11. Extension path (optional)

This is optional and only runs if county-level finance data already exists.

finance_panel_path <- "data/public/derived/county_tariff_panel_with_campaign_finance.csv"

if (!file.exists(finance_panel_path)) {
  cat(
    "Campaign-finance extension data is not present yet.\n\n",
    "To build it:\n",
    "1) source('code/add_campaign_finance_county_measures.R')\n",
    "2) Make sure FEC_API_KEY is set, or cached ZIP-level files exist in data/public/raw/\n",
    "3) Re-knit this workbook\n"
  )
} else {
  finance_panel <- read_csv(finance_panel_path, show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
    mutate(
      county_fips = str_pad(as.character(county_fips), 5, pad = "0"),
      year = as.integer(year)
    )

  needed <- c(
    "county_fips", "year", "county_tariff_exposure_mean", "gop_share_20",
    "swingness_20", "tariffed_emp_share", "state_name",
    "donations_total", "spending_total"
  )
  missing_needed <- setdiff(needed, names(finance_panel))

  if (length(missing_needed) > 0) {
    cat(
      "Finance panel is present, but some required columns are missing:\n",
      paste0(" - ", missing_needed, collapse = "\n"),
      "\nPlease rebuild with code/add_campaign_finance_county_measures.R\n"
    )
  } else {
    latest_finance_year <- max(finance_panel$year, na.rm = TRUE)
    fin_latest <- finance_panel %>% filter(year == latest_finance_year)

    fin_diag <- fin_latest %>%
      summarise(
        year = latest_finance_year,
        counties = n_distinct(county_fips),
        counties_with_donations = sum(donations_total > 0, na.rm = TRUE),
        counties_with_spending = sum(spending_total > 0, na.rm = TRUE),
        median_donations = median(donations_total, na.rm = TRUE),
        median_spending = median(spending_total, na.rm = TRUE)
      )

    kable(fin_diag, digits = 2, caption = "Campaign-finance data coverage (latest year)")

    reg_fin <- fin_latest %>%
      mutate(
        y = log1p(county_tariff_exposure_mean),
        ln_donations = log1p(donations_total),
        ln_spending = log1p(spending_total)
      ) %>%
      filter(
        is.finite(y),
        is.finite(ln_donations),
        is.finite(ln_spending),
        !is.na(gop_share_20),
        !is.na(swingness_20),
        !is.na(tariffed_emp_share),
        !is.na(state_name),
        state_name != ""
      )

    m_base <- feols(
      y ~ gop_share_20 + swingness_20 + tariffed_emp_share | state_name,
      data = reg_fin,
      vcov = ~state_name
    )

    m_fin <- feols(
      y ~ gop_share_20 + swingness_20 + tariffed_emp_share + ln_donations + ln_spending | state_name,
      data = reg_fin,
      vcov = ~state_name
    )

    modelsummary(
      list(
        "Baseline FE + clustered" = m_base,
        "Add donations/spending controls" = m_fin
      ),
      stars = FALSE,
      fmt = 3,
      output = "markdown"
    )
  }
}
## Campaign-finance extension data is not present yet.
## 
##  To build it:
##  1) source('code/add_campaign_finance_county_measures.R')
##  2) Make sure FEC_API_KEY is set, or cached ZIP-level files exist in data/public/raw/
##  3) Re-knit this workbook

Teaching takeaway

“This does not replace your main question. It pressure-tests whether the core pattern survives when campaign-finance context is added.”

12. Closing checkpoint

Before ending this workbook, answer these clearly out loud:

  • What is the shift term in this project?
  • What is the share term in this project?
  • Why are customs districts not enough for production-side local exposure?
  • Why do we report both level and per-worker exposure?
  • Why does clustered inference matter for county data?

13. Final note

Jubili, this is the part that matters most: your core research idea was strong from day one.

What you now have is that same idea with tighter measurement, cleaner diagnostics, and more defensible interpretation.

That is how good student work becomes professional work.
